subject: ap-precalculus
title: Sinusoidal Modeling: Ocean Tides & Periodic Water Depth
stimulus: At a coastal harbor, the depth of the water changes periodically due to tides. On a particular day, the water reaches its maximum depth of $14$ feet at $2{:}00$ AM and its minimum depth of $6$ feet at $8{:}00$ AM. The sinusoidal function $D$ models the depth of the water, in feet, as a function of time $t$, in hours after midnight.

parts.prompt:
The function $D$ can be written in the form $D(t) = a\cos(b(t - c)) + d$.

(i) Find the values of constants $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$.

(ii) Explain the meaning of the value of $d$ in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: Amplitude $a = (14-6)/2 = 4$; midline $d = (14+6)/2 = 10$; half-period from max to min is $8 - 2 = 6$ hours, so period $= 12$, giving $b = 2\pi/12 = \pi/6$; cosine reaches maximum at $t = c$, so $c = 2$. Accept $a = -4$ with $c = 8$ (minimum as reference). For part (ii), the explanation must reference the context (water depth) and the midline meaning. Stating only '$d$ is the vertical shift' without contextual meaning does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 4$, $d = 10$, $b = \frac{\pi}{6}$, $c = 2$; accept $a = -4$ with $c = 8$ (minimum as reference point)
wrong_examples: $b = 2\pi/6 = \pi/3$ (using half-period instead of full period for period formula)
common_traps: Amplitude is half the range: $(14 - 6)/2 = 4$, not $8$
Period is $12$ hours (max to next max), not $6$ hours (max to min which is only half-period)

scoring_points.description: $d = 10$ represents the average (midline) depth of the water — the depth halfway between the maximum and minimum; explanation must reference the context of water depth

alternatives.correct_example: $d = 10$ feet represents the average depth of the water at the harbor — the midline of the tidal variation, halfway between the maximum depth of $14$ feet and the minimum depth of $6$ feet.

wrong_examples: '$d$ is the vertical shift' without contextual meaning
common_traps: Must connect $d = 10$ to a real-world meaning about average or midline water depth — a purely mathematical statement is insufficient

parts.prompt:
(i) Find all times $t$, in hours after midnight, where $0 \leq t < 24$, at which the water depth equals $10$ feet. Express exact values.

(ii) On the interval where $2 < t < 8$, describe whether $D$ is increasing or decreasing, and describe the concavity of the graph of $D$.
parts.scoring_notes: Setting $D = 10$ gives $\cos(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2)) = 0$, so $\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2) = \frac{\pi}{2} + k\pi$; solving: $t = 5, 11, 17, 23$ in $[0, 24)$. All four values required. For part (ii), $D$ is decreasing on $(2, 8)$ (from max to min). Concavity changes at $t = 5$ (inflection at midline): concave down on $(2, 5)$ (rate of decrease accelerating) and concave up on $(5, 8)$ (rate of decrease decelerating). Stating only 'concave up' or only 'concave down' without noting the change does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: All four correct times $t = 5, 11, 17, 23$ hours after midnight, with no incorrect values
wrong_examples: Only $t = 5$ and $t = 11$ (missing the second cycle values)
Including $t = 2$ or $t = 8$ (these are the max/min, not midline crossings)
common_traps: There are four midline crossings per day (two per cycle, two full cycles in 24 hours) — missing any of the four does not earn the point

scoring_points.description: $D$ is decreasing on $(2, 8)$; concavity changes: concave down on $(2, 5)$ and concave up on $(5, 8)$, with inflection at $t = 5$ (the midline crossing)

required_elements: concave down on $(2, 5)$ AND concave up on $(5, 8)$
alternatives.correct_example: $D$ is decreasing on $(2, 8)$ (from maximum $14$ ft to minimum $6$ ft). The concavity changes at $t = 5$: concave down on $(2, 5)$ (the rate of decrease is increasing) and concave up on $(5, 8)$ (the rate of decrease is slowing as it approaches the minimum).

wrong_examples: Stating only 'decreasing and concave down' (misses the concavity change)
common_traps: The inflection point is at the midline crossing $t = 5$, halfway between max and min — concavity changes there, not at the max or min

parts.prompt: A fishing boat can only safely enter the harbor when the water depth is at least $11$ feet. Determine all time intervals during $0 \leq t < 24$ when the boat can safely enter the harbor. Express exact values.
parts.scoring_notes: Setting $D \geq 11$ gives $4\cos(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2)) \geq 1$, so $\cos(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2)) \geq 1/4$. Critical times: $\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2) = \pm\arccos(1/4)$, so $t = 2 \pm \frac{6}{\pi}\arccos(1/4) \approx 2 \pm 2.52$. First safe window: $[-0.52, 4.52]$ — since $t \geq 0$, this becomes $[0, 4.52)$. Second window centered at $t = 14$: $(11.48, 16.52)$. Third partial window: $(23.48, 24)$. Decimal approximations acceptable. Partial credit for correct setup without all three intervals.

scoring_points.description: Correct setup leading to critical times $t \approx 4.52$ and $t \approx -0.52$ (or $23.48$) from $\cos(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-2)) \geq \frac{1}{4}$
wrong_examples: Setting $D = 10$ instead of $D = 11$ (solving for midline crossing instead of safe threshold)
common_traps: The inequality direction: cosine $\geq 1/4$ (not $\leq$) corresponds to depth $\geq 11$ ft since cosine is the function value relative to amplitude

scoring_points.description: All three correct safe-entry intervals within $[0, 24)$: approximately $[0, 4.52)$, $(11.48, 16.52)$, and $(23.48, 24)$
wrong_examples: Only listing two intervals (forgetting the wrap-around interval near $t = 23.48$)
Listing intervals where depth is less than $11$ ft (inverted inequality)
common_traps: The cycle straddles midnight: the first safe window starts before $t = 0$, so within $[0, 24)$ it begins at $t = 0$; the last safe window wraps around to just before $t = 24$
